footer.description: An essential guide to reading the glacial landscape of Antigorio Valley, one step at a time.
footer.disclaimer: Always check trail conditions, weather and local information before visiting.

meta.title: Uriezzo Gorges and the Giant’s Potholes
meta.description: Explore two glacial landscapes in Antigorio Valley with practical guides, multilingual audio and visitor information.

home.title: Two landscapes carved by water
home.intro: Between granite walls, damp woodland and the Toce River, the trail tells a story of ice, whirlpools and rock spanning thousands of years. Choose where to begin.
home.trailNote: The South Gorge and the Maiesso potholes are connected, so both can be visited on the same walk when time and conditions allow.

meta.title: Uriezzo Gorges: history, geology and visit
meta.description: Complete guide to the Uriezzo Gorges in Antigorio Valley: glacial origins, routes, access from Baceno, Premia and Verampio, safety and real photographs.

orridi.heroAlt: Sinuous walls and a narrow passage inside the Uriezzo Gorges
orridi.title: Uriezzo Gorges
orridi.subtitle: An ancient network of subglacial streams transformed into a dry canyon that can be explored on foot, among polished granite, moss and shafts of light.
orridi.introEyebrow: Inside the memory of the ice
intro: Entering the Uriezzo Gorges means walking through a landscape that no longer belongs to the valley’s present drainage system. The passages are now almost dry, yet their walls preserve the shape and force of streams that once flowed beneath and beside the great Toce Glacier. The result is an intimate landscape of narrow corridors, rounded chambers, niches, grooves and sudden blades of light.

The complex lies in Antigorio Valley between Baceno, Premia and the Verampio area. The South Gorge, locally known as the “Tomb of Uriezzo”, is the most monumental; the North-East Gorge is shorter and narrower; the West Gorge has less straightforward passages and calls for more experience. Vallaccia, the fourth gorge, is difficult to reach and is not suitable for an ordinary visit.

These gorges are more than a geological curiosity. They reveal the relationship between water, stone and Alpine communities: access routes follow old mule tracks, abandoned terraces and links between hamlets. Silence, humidity and vegetation clinging to the walls turn the walk into a close reading of the natural history of the Ossola Valley.

arrival.title: Getting there and finding your way
body: The most direct and usually recommended access is from the Premia side. The road toward Crego and Uriezzo leads to the Oratory of Santa Lucia; from there continue on foot, following hiking signs and yellow arrows. The North-East Gorge comes first, while the South Gorge is reached by continuing downhill along the unpaved track to the marked entrance.

From Baceno, the route begins near the monumental church of San Gaudenzio. A mule track, partly paved and partly cut into the rock, descends toward Verampio. At the signed junction, take the path crossing terraced woodland and reaching the service track beside the South Gorge. It is a scenic approach, but adds elevation change and time compared with Premia.

From the Verampio valley floor, one route starts near Trattoria della Campagna and follows the track across the Devero stream, beside the Toce and toward Maiesso Bridge. Here you can see the Giant’s Potholes before continuing to the South Gorge. Times vary with access point, stops and conditions, so keep a sensible margin.

geology.title: Glacial history and the making of the gorges
body: During the last major glaciation, which ended roughly twelve thousand years ago, Antigorio Valley was occupied by the Toce Glacier. Meltwater under and along the ice flowed under strong pressure, carrying sand, gravel and pebbles that could cut the granite like a natural file.

The water did not carve a straight channel. Whirlpools, drops and changes in direction enlarged existing fractures, producing rounded chambers, winding passages and undulating walls. Niches that now look hand-carved resulted from prolonged erosion: every grain carried by the current helped polish, widen and deepen the route.

As the climate warmed, the glacier retreated and the drainage system changed. Streams abandoned these incisions, leaving fossil gorges without the water that had created them. This exceptional condition makes it possible to walk inside and inspect shapes that would be hidden beneath the current in an active gorge.

visit.title: Environment, seasons and safety
body: The gorges have a microclimate distinct from the surrounding woodland. Light enters mainly from above, humidity remains high, and mosses and ferns thrive on the shaded walls. After rain the colours intensify, but the floor and metal steps can become slippery.

Spring and autumn are generally the most pleasant seasons. From late March into May the landscape reopens after winter and wild cherry trees may flower nearby; autumn brings warm woodland colours against grey granite. Winter visits are discouraged because of ice and the risk of falling fragments.

Wear hiking shoes with reliable grip, carry a warm layer even in summer and supervise children on narrow or equipped sections. Do not enter during thunderstorms, heavy rain, ice or local closures. The West Gorge and Vallaccia should not be treated as a casual tourist stroll: assess experience, conditions and signs on site.

highlights.title: Reading the gorge system
items: South Gorge, or “Tomb of Uriezzo”: the most dramatic section, about 200 metres long and up to 20–30 metres deep.
North-East Gorge: about 100 metres, narrow and enclosed, with walls that reveal the polishing process at close range.
West Gorge: less monumental but more demanding; some passages require care and are assisted by a rope.
Vallaccia: the fourth gorge, difficult to access and not recommended for visitors without suitable experience.

orridi.narration: The Uriezzo Gorges are fossil canyons in Antigorio Valley, created by meltwater flowing beneath the Toce Glacier during the last glaciation. Sand, gravel and pebbles carried at high energy cut winding corridors, chambers and polished walls into the granite. When the glacier retreated around twelve thousand years ago, the streams changed course and left the gorges dry enough to explore. The South Gorge, known as the Tomb of Uriezzo, is the most spectacular; the North-East Gorge is shorter and narrower, while the West Gorge requires greater experience. Main access points are Premia, Baceno and Verampio. Good footwear and caution are essential because the floor may be damp, metal steps are present and winter ice makes visits inadvisable.

meta.title: Giant’s Potholes at Maiesso: history and visit
meta.description: Complete guide to the Giant’s Potholes on the Toce at Maiesso: glacial origin, vortex erosion, connection with the gorges and safety advice.

marmitte.heroAlt: Giant’s Potholes and polished rocks along the Toce River at Maiesso
marmitte.title: Giant’s Potholes
marmitte.subtitle: Large circular hollows in granite, carved by meltwater, whirlpools and sediment carried by the Toce.
marmitte.introEyebrow: When the river becomes a sculptor
intro: At Maiesso the Toce crosses compact rock in which water has opened bowls, cylinders and rounded hollows. These are the Giant’s Potholes: striking forms that resemble deliberately carved vessels but are the result of natural processes lasting thousands of years.

Their name belongs to Alpine folklore, where cavities of this scale were attributed to giants. The geological explanation is just as compelling: turbulent water, sand and pebbles rotated in persistent eddies, turning sediment into abrasive tools and progressively deepening the granite.

The potholes are a natural continuation of a visit to the Uriezzo Gorges. Leaving the South Gorge and walking toward Maiesso Bridge takes you from a dry fossil canyon to an active river environment. Within a short walk, two different chapters of the same glacial story become visible.

arrival.title: Getting there and combining both visits
body: The best-known viewpoint is near Maiesso Bridge on the Toce. From the Oratory of Santa Lucia and the gorge accesses, follow the route to the South Gorge; after crossing it, continue downhill to the bridge. This links the fossil canyon and the active river forms in a single walk.

From Baceno, descend on the mule track near San Gaudenzio church and continue toward Verampio and Maiesso. From the Verampio valley floor, the track follows the Devero stream and the Toce to the bridge, clearly showing the relationship between present waterways and the landscape shaped by ice.

The typical return time from Santa Lucia is about one hour and ten minutes when two gorges and Maiesso are included, without long stops. Allow more time for photographs, observation and crowds, and always check signs for diversions or restrictions.

geology.title: Water, whirlpools and glacial memory
body: The potholes developed especially when glacial meltwater supplied very high flows. Water accelerated through constrained granite channels and formed eddies. Where the current began to rotate, sand and pebbles became trapped and repeatedly followed circular paths.

Erosion depended not only on the force of water but also on the rock fragments it carried. Pebbles struck and polished the bottom, while finer grains wore down the walls. Over time, a small irregularity grew into a deep, often cylindrical hollow, with smooth surfaces and grooves recording the current’s movement.

Maiesso reveals the difference between the broad work of the glacier, which enlarged and organised the valley, and the finer work of meltwater, which carved details little by little. Nearby, the Crego hydroelectric station, designed by Piero Portaluppi between 1917 and 1926, adds a modern chapter: people began to harness the same water energy that had shaped the rock.

visit.title: Observation and safety beside the Toce
body: The potholes are viewed in an active river setting. Even when the water appears calm, flow can change and polished granite becomes extremely slippery. Stay on safe paths and viewpoints; do not step onto wet rocks or enter the hollows.

Wear shoes with a gripping sole, keep children close and do not cross barriers or signs. After heavy rain, thunderstorms or rapid snowmelt the Toce can rise; in such conditions, turn back or remain only at officially accessible viewpoints.

Low-angle light makes the depth of hollows and tones of granite easier to read. Do not focus solely on photographs, however: irregular ground requires constant attention. A slow visit reveals smooth surfaces, grooves, trapped pebbles and the relationship between bridge, river and the exit of the South Gorge.

highlights.title: What to observe at Maiesso
items: Hemispherical and cylindrical hollows formed where eddies kept sand and pebbles rotating.
Polished surfaces and granite grooves recording the direction and intensity of abrasion.
Maiesso Bridge, the visual and walking link between the Toce, the potholes and the South Gorge exit.
The Crego power station, evidence of how Alpine water energy was converted into electricity in the twentieth century.

marmitte.narration: The Giant’s Potholes lie at Maiesso along the Toce River and share a trail with the Uriezzo Gorges. These rounded and cylindrical hollows were carved into granite by glacial meltwater. Sand and pebbles trapped in eddies rotated like abrasive tools, polishing and deepening the rock for thousands of years. From Maiesso Bridge you can see the relationship between the present river and the exit of the South Gorge. Wet granite is very slippery and the Toce can change quickly, so remain at safe viewpoints, supervise children and turn back during floods or thunderstorms. Nearby stands the Crego hydroelectric station, designed by Piero Portaluppi between 1917 and 1926.
